steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The first sentence states a general challenge: biographers of pre-nineteenth-century subjects struggle with scarce sources. The second sentence describes Ron Chernow having access to 26 volumes and 19,000 documents. The transition must signal that Chernow's situation is the opposite of that scarcity.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — To take one well-known example,: frames Chernow as an example of the scarcity problem, but he actually had abundant sources — the opposite. ✗
  • Choice B — In accordance with this premise,: says Chernow's situation agrees with the scarcity premise, but 19,000 documents contradicts scarcity. ✗
  • Choice C — It thus came as no surprise that,: implies abundance naturally follows from scarcity, which is illogical. ✗
  • Choice D — On the other end of the spectrum,: explicitly marks Chernow's case as the contrasting extreme — abundant sources versus the general problem of scarce sources. ✓
D is correct.
